dueBasis: Backflow assemblies enrolled in the local cross-connection program must be tested annually and submitted through the utility workflow.
coveredPropertyTypes: Multifamily and facility sites enrolled by the utility
officialListLabel: Open the Grand Prairie program page
penalties: Unresolved failures can trigger utility enforcement and service-risk follow-up under the local program.
sourceExcerpt: Grand Prairie describes a cross-connection control and prevention program that requires annual testing and documentation for enrolled backflow prevention assemblies.
verdictSummary: Annual testing is required for enrolled backflow prevention assemblies in the Grand Prairie utility program.
whoIsAffected: Commercial sites, irrigation systems, and other hazard connections managed through the Grand Prairie cross-connection program.
residentialNotes: Residential pressure vacuum breakers and irrigation assemblies matter mainly when the utility or irrigation rules require enrollment.
Homeowners should confirm whether a pool auto-fill or irrigation setup has already triggered a backflow record with the City.
commercialNotes: Grand Prairie's cross-connection program is more likely to touch commercial hazards, multifamily sites, and managed facilities.
Treat annual testing and documentation as part of the utility compliance file, not just a plumbing service call.

annualTesting.summary: Grand Prairie uses a straightforward annual testing model for assemblies enrolled in the cross-connection program.
highlights: Annual testing is the default expectation for enrolled assemblies.
The utility workflow matters as much as the field test itself because the documentation still has to be accepted.
This page is strongest for users who already know they are in the program and need the next action path.
workflowSteps: Confirm the device is in Grand Prairie's cross-connection program.
Schedule the annual test before the utility deadline window closes.
Submit the report through the utility's accepted process and keep proof of submission.
If the device fails, complete repair and retest before the utility follow-up window closes.

irrigation.summary: Grand Prairie irrigation assemblies are part of the same utility-controlled cross-connection program when the site requires an enrolled device.
highlights: Do not assume an irrigation assembly is outside the program just because the property is smaller.
Irrigation, pools, and other outdoor hazards can still trigger the same annual documentation problem.
Use the utility program page first when a landscaper gives a generic answer.
workflowSteps: Confirm whether the irrigation setup requires an enrolled backflow assembly under the utility program.
Schedule testing with a tester who can submit through the accepted utility path.
If the device fails, repair and retest before treating the file as complete.
Confirm the assembly is enrolled in the utility cross-connection program.
Schedule the annual test with a tester recognized by the governing workflow.
Submit the report through the utility's accepted process and keep proof of submission.
If the assembly fails, complete repairs and arrange a retest before the deadline.
failureHighlights: A failed assembly typically needs repair plus a documented retest.
Do not treat repair completion alone as closure; the retest still matters.
If the utility requests correction quickly, use the official program page first.

costBand.testingRange: $85 to $165 for a routine annual test
costBand.repairRetestRange: $250 to $1,250 depending on device type and parts
costBand.pricingNotes: Commercial hazard class, emergency scheduling, and device accessibility change the final price.
